text: The surgeon identifies the extent of the bladder and prepares to dissect, ensuring careful navigation to avoid opening the bladder during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder, applying steady traction to separate it from surrounding tissues, ensuring precise manipulation within the identified anatomical plane.
The surgeon reaches the under surface of the bladder, holding it in place while carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum and makes an incision, being cautious of the bladder's extent to avoid opening it.
The surgeon exposes the bladder wall, which is now visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon retroverts the bladder, applying traction to reposition it and improve access for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the bladder and visible fibers in front of the surgical field. The cervix is noted, and the surgeon proceeds to cut the tissue at the lateral boundary.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the bladder, with the surrounding anatomical structures now clearly visible.
